videos.title: Markets React To Brexit
videos.text: AND POSSIBLE DANGER IN THE WATER. WHY SOME DAYCARE CENTERS MAY NOT BE AS SAFE AS YOU THINK. IT'S NOT WHAT YOU WANT TO HEAR IF YOU HAVE MONEY IN THE MARKET. WALL STREET TOOK A BEATING TODAY. BRITAIN'S DECISION TO LEAVE THE EUROPEAN UNION SENT STOCKS INTO A TAILSPIN ACROSS THE GLOBE. THE DOW SUFFERING ITS 8th LARGEST POINT LOSS EVER ENDING THE DAY DOWN 611 POINTS. SO WHAT'S NEXT FOR THE WORLD ECONOMY? CBS 2'S TONY AIELLO REPORTS FROM OUTSIDE THE NEW YORK STOCK EXCHANGE. Reporter: POLLS SAID THE UK WOULD VOTE TO REMAIN IN THE EU. THE POLLS WERE WRONG. AND WORLD MARKETS REACTED WITH SHOCK. IT WAS A GOOD DAY FOR SAFE HAVENS INCLUDING GOLD AND U.S. TREASURY BONDS. A BAD DAY FOR FINANCIAL FIRMS. CITIGROUP STOCK, FOR EXAMPLE, DOWN MORE THAN 9%. MANY IN THE U.S. SEE THE VOLATILITY AND WONDER WHERE TO STASH THEIR CASH. I BASICALLY HIDE IT UNDER MY BED. [ LAUGHTER ] HOLD MY BREATH AND RIDE IT OUT BUT IT'S SCARY. Reporter: THERE'S NO ONE SIZE FITS ALL ADVICE TO DEAL WITH MARKET VOLATILITY OTHER THAN THAT AGE-OLD ADDAGE, ASSESS YOUR TOLERANCE FOR RISK AND INVEST ACCORDINGLY. ENGLAND IS THE WORLD'S FIFTH LARGEST ECONOMY. ITS VOTE TO LEAVE THE EU IS UNPRECEDENTED AND NO ONE CAN SAY FOR SURE HOW IT WILL ALL PLAY OUT. ONE THING THAT PEOPLE NEED TO BEAR IN MIND IS THAT WE'RE NOT FLIPPING A SWITCH TODAY AND ALL OF A SUDDEN THE UK IS OUT OF THE EU. THIS WILL BE A LONG PROCESS. Reporter: BUT EVEN IN THE MIDDLE OF UNCERTAINTY, THERE'S OPPORTUNITY. THE BRITISH POUND HAS TUMBLED AGAINST THE DOLLAR. THIS TRAVEL EXPERT SAYS THIS WILL BE A GREAT SUMMER TO CROSS THE POND. THE FLIGHTS ARE CHEAPER. THE HOTELS ARE CHEAPER. THE GOODS ARE CHEAPER. THERE HAS NEVER BEEN A CHEAPER TIME TO TAKE THAT DREAM VACATION TO LONDON. Reporter: BUT THAT VERY DYNAMIC WILL MAKE NEW YORK CITY MORE EXPENSIVE FOR VISITORS FROM THE UK WHICH

videos.text: another interesting session here in Asia certainly if you look at assets which are directly and to degree in directly exposed to what we're seeing in the UK referendum was in a continuation of the most resort from Friday so certainly the Sterling coming out the blocks pretty hard there's been a lot of concern about what's happening in you tight UK politics will we see or at what stage will we see an enactment of article 15 will that potentially lead to early sir this messy divorce from the EU effectively so people are can continuing to asking questions around what is the actual plan around that there's big concerns about what's happening with UK growth we've seen a number of investment banks cutting growth to something like 20 basis points for 2017 there's talk that they are the Bank of England will have to cut rates that the swaps market pricing in a sixty percent chance of that coming through by the end of this year and there's even thought that we could see quantitative easing being reenacted in the UK and certainly hard not to mention the fact that the Labour Party seems to be imploding around Jeremy Corbyn as well so all these uncertainties breathing fervice weakness in the sterling sterling against the greenback down about two percent at the moment footsie futures coming out the block down about three percent but they have been lower on the day and I'll certainly our opening calls for European markets have been lower than where they are at the moment we are seeing some buying coming into the SP and footsy and dow dow futures as well gold price is continuing to see further hires in fact if you price them in Australian dollars they're actually not a they're only 35 handles away from being an alternate new all-time high which i think is quite an interesting situation there as well but what we are seeing there is is the concern about what's happening there has reverberated in those specific assets but I think as well you've also seen a big move in the US dollar on Friday and the implications here for Asia is that the emerging markets are underperforming we're seeing good selling coming and emerging market currencies worth keeping an eye on the eem which is the emerging market ETF and China because of that strength that we saw had one of the biggest fixings they've had in a long time so the actual dollar against the sea my rate is now trading at the highest level since 2010 we go back to January where there was a big macro concern for markets so again what happens if the US dollar continues to find safe haven flows from that will put a lot of stress on emerging markets and that could be something that could trouble markets going forward it's not just about the UK referent referendum it's about where the capital flows go so we're having a look at what happens with the US dollar and what ultimately happens there for the S&P and inflation expectations coming through but what we've seen today here in Asia is a big rejection of these moves that we've been seeing in footsie an SMP futures the data the neck I looking really strong at the moment we've got the asx200 up about 80 basis points as well so some safe haven buying in some of the telcos in fact mining stocks are doing quite well as well today so a bit of an interesting breakdown in correlations between markets but certainly there are some worrying themes that we're seeing in markets driven by what's happened from the UK referendum and what that is exposed for European banking sector and also for dollar inflows as well you

videos.title: 23rd of June 2016 � Markets Hold Their Breath Ahead of BREXIT Vote.
videos.text: hi this is Steve Knight and welcome to Blackwell Global's daily market update starting with the euro dollar and the pair managed to catch a bed late in the session as the latest UK brexit polls continue to narrow suggesting that the leave votes lead has largely evaporated subsequently the Euro rallied strongly to currently trade just above the key one spot 13 handle however there are still plenty of uncertainty around and the Euro will likely remain relatively volatile until the vote is finally settled also on the fundamentals front keep a close watch on the pending EU manufacturing PMI figures turning to the tumultuous cable now in Judgment day is finally upon us as the UK prepares for the historic vote on their EU membership both sides have campaigned furiously in the past few days and the vote now remains too close to call in addition even the EU has sought to influence the vote with jeanclaude Yonker suggesting that out is out and no New Deals will be available for the UK subsequently given that the outcome is currently impossible to predict expect to see plenty of sharp moves on Game Day finally turning to the Australian dollar and the pair Rose strongly against the greenb is part of a broadly negative US dollar sentiment which has swept markets overnight much of the current Capital flows due to repositioning and taking a risk-off approach ahead of the expected brexit volatility subsequently the Aussie dollar was a net beneficiary and ried to finish the session around the 75 cent handle moving forward keep our watch on the US unemployment claims figures due shortly well that's all I've got for you today this is Steve Knight with Blackwell Global thanks for watching

videos.text: This is not a move. This is an adjustment. There's a big difference. The UK is one of the biggest current count deficits in the world. The UK has a massive fiscal deficit. What needs to adjust is the currency. Part of the issue of other countries, Greece for example, is it doesn't have a currency to adjust. The real economy has to adjust. Here the currency is the adjustment mechanism. It needs to fall to a level at which the UK gets investment back again. and I don't think the fall is over. Uh earlier this week, George Soros said that the because of the current account deficit, a big fall in sterling was a very bad thing for the UK economy. Do you agree? Well, who might to argue with the great man? Um yeah, but it's not the falling sterling that's the bad thing. It's the consequences of a decision that we've made now. Um we've got a very big decision we've made. We've got a very big current account. We got a big fiscal deficit and the currency needs to adjust and perhaps domestic demand has to adjust as well. Wow. That's why nobody expects the Bank of England to step in because and we need the adjustment. It's an adjustment that has to take place and fortunately for us in the UK, we've got a currency that can take a lot of the flat, but I'm afraid the economy will weaken as well.
